narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model predicted a 56.6% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application—it's a tough outcome, but understanding why can help you move forward. Your profile shows strong points, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.50 that's above the typical 3.21 average, along with middle family income, being a full-time student, female, and White—demographics that align closely with many successful applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 and especially your Year 3 decile of 1 are quite low compared to averages around 5.5-5.7, and your LSAT score of 30 falls well below the usual 36.7. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 performance, which had the strongest influence pushing the prediction toward failure since it signals potential struggles in later academics. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also driving the risk higher due to its below-average mark. Your Year 1 decile ranked third, similarly contributing to concerns about early academic consistency. Family income came fourth, with your middle bracket nudging the odds slightly against you in this context, followed by your UGPA, race, gender, and full-time status, all of which played smaller roles but still leaned toward higher failure risk for your specific combination. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower deciles and LSAT stand out as bigger drags than most, while your UGPA is a relative bright spot that wasn't enough to offset them. To boost your bar exam chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score through targeted prep, as that's fully within your control and highly influential. Building better study habits to improve academic deciles in future coursework could also help demonstrate growth. Consider resources like bar prep courses early, regardless of income, to address patterns from your deciles. With these steps, you can reshape your profile and reapply stronger—many students turn predictions like this around. Keep pushing; your solid UGPA shows you have the foundation.
